us-politics.headline: Iran Downs US Jets as Hormuz Crisis Deepens
us-politics.body:
Iran shot down American fighter jets this week, prompting sharp criticism from both parties. Rep. Seth Moulton warned that Iran is winning the broader conflict, while former National Security Advisor John Bolton suggested Trump may be in panic mode. The incidents mark a serious escalation in an already volatile standoff.

World leaders are moving to address the Strait of Hormuz crisis largely without US involvement, raising questions about Washington's diplomatic standing. Critics on the left see the administration as reactive and isolated. Supporters argue Trump's pressure campaign is forcing a reckoning. Either way, the US has lost aircraft and lost the room.

sources.title: Moulton says ‘Iran is winning the war’ after US jets downed
Bolton: Trump likely in ‘panic mode’ after Iran attacks US fighter jets
World leaders bypass Trump to tackle Strait of Hormuz crisis

us-politics.headline: Cabinet Shake-Ups Signal Turbulence Inside Trump White House
us-politics.body:
The departures of Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem from the Cabinet have fuelled speculation about further shake-ups inside the Trump administration. Both exits were abrupt, and neither came with a clear public explanation from the White House.

NPR's political roundup noted the removals as part of a broader pattern of instability at senior levels. Analysts across the spectrum are watching whether Trump consolidates loyalists or continues rotating officials. The question now is who is next - and whether the churn reflects strategic reshaping or simple dysfunction.

sources.title: Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi
Bondi, Noem ousters raise questions about future Cabinet shake-ups

us-politics.headline: Birthright Citizenship Battle Heads Toward Constitutional Showdown
us-politics.body:
The Supreme Court is weighing the Trump administration's push to restrict birthright citizenship, a move that has reignited a long-running constitutional debate. A separate opinion piece in The Hill argues the Fourteenth Amendment may need revisiting, reflecting a view gaining traction on the right that current interpretation is too broad.

Critics, including many constitutional scholars and Democrats, say the amendment's language is unambiguous and any executive action to limit it is unlawful. A separate case involving a child of Chinese immigrants charged with planting explosives at a military base has added political heat to the immigration debate, with conservative outlets highlighting it prominently.

sources.title: Reclaiming our own birthright: We might need to amend the Constitution
Child of Chinese illegal immigrants charged with planting explosive at US military base
Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi

us-politics.headline: Trump Pushes College Sports Order, Alcatraz Plan Amid Policy Blitz
us-politics.body:
President Trump signed an executive order aimed at reforming college sports, but Sen. Richard Blumenthal dismissed it as theater that sidesteps the real issues facing student athletes. The order drew bipartisan skepticism from those who say only Congress can deliver lasting change.

Separately, the White House has requested $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison, a proposal Trump floated publicly earlier this year. He also suggested shifting day care policy to the states, a move child care advocates called unworkable. Taken together, the moves reflect a governing style heavy on bold announcements and light on legislative follow-through - a pattern supporters call disruptive and critics call hollow.

sources.title: Blumenthal: Trump’s order to reform college sports ‘is theater, not a fix’
Alcatraz could reopen as a 'state-of-the-art secure prison' under Trump's $152M budget request
Trump floats letting states handle day care. Advocates call it a lead balloon

au-politics.headline: Murder charges, missing women dominate crime headlines nationwide
au-politics.body:
Victorian police arrested two people in connection with the disappearance of Dezi Freeman, while a man was charged with murder after being intercepted attempting to board an international flight. A separate murder charge was laid against a woman in Laverton following the death of a 24-year-old. Three distinct cases, all breaking within 24 hours.

In Western Australia, a full-scale search is underway for a woman missing near The Gap in Albany, a popular tourism site. Police have not yet linked that case to any criminal conduct.

The cluster of cases puts pressure on state police forces already managing complex investigations. No trials have been scheduled and all accused are presumed innocent.

sources.title: Man trying to board international flight charged with murder
Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
Police launch full-scale search to locate woman missing in popular tourism spot
Two arrested as part of investigation into movements of Dezi Freeman
Woman charged with murder following investigation into death of 24yo

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo report reveals 'reckless' conduct with Liberal powerbroker
au-politics.body:
A confidential report found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his dealings with a Liberal powerbroker, adding detail to the conduct that led to his sacking in 2023. The report's existence and findings have not previously been made public.

Pezzullo was dismissed after text messages revealed he had discussed ministerial appointments and political strategy with Liberal operative Scott Briggs over several years. The new report suggests the behaviour was more serious than the public record indicated.

The findings raise fresh questions about the boundary between senior public servants and political operatives - and whether existing accountability mechanisms caught the problem early enough.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Victorian premier faces direct challenge ahead of state election
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean will contest Jacinta Allan's seat of Bendigo East at the next Victorian state election, setting up a direct challenge to the premier in her own electorate. Allan has held the seat since 2002 and succeeded Daniel Andrews as premier in 2023.

The move follows a pattern of opposition parties targeting safe seats held by senior government figures to force premiers onto the defensive locally. Whether Lethlean poses a genuine threat or is primarily a tactical distraction remains to be seen. Allan's government has faced sustained criticism over cost-of-living pressures and infrastructure spending.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

au-politics.headline: Aged care CEO pay hits $679k as fuel supply concerns ease
au-politics.body:
Queensland residential aged care CEOs are earning up to $679,000 annually, a financial review has found, reigniting debate about executive pay in a sector that relies heavily on public funding. Critics argue the figures are difficult to justify given ongoing workforce shortages and care quality concerns across the sector.

Separately, the federal energy minister says fewer service stations are running out of petrol and diesel, describing Australia's fuel supply as stable. The reassurance follows weeks of concern about supply chain resilience, though the Spectator has argued the government's broader energy policy reflects what it calls a pattern of ideological overreach rather than practical management.

sources.title: The West’s parasite politics
Qld residential care sector CEOs paid up to $679k
Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Just, you know, check your tyre pressure

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates as Nuclear Plant Hit
world.body:
A projectile struck near Iran's Bushehr nuclear plant on day 36 of US-Israeli military operations, killing one person, according to the IAEA. Debris from Iranian projectiles also hit vehicles inside Israel, signalling the conflict is now running in both directions with growing intensity.

Iran executed two members of a banned opposition group amid the fighting, a move critics say is designed to suppress dissent under cover of war. A US airman remains missing inside Iran, with rescue teams describing the search as harrowing and dangerous. The conflict shows no sign of a ceiling.

sources.title: Vehicles burn in Israel after being hit with Iranian projectile debris
Projectile hits near Iran’s Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one: IAEA
What we know so far about the search for missing US airman in Iran
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group
Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?

world.headline: Russia Kills Five at Ukraine Market Over Easter Weekend
world.body:
Russian strikes hit a Ukrainian market, killing five people, as separate attacks across the country killed at least four more. President Zelensky accused Russia of choosing an Easter escalation over the ceasefire framework that had been under discussion.

The strikes mark one of the deadlier single-day tolls in recent weeks. Russia has not commented on Zelensky's ceasefire claim.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five
At least four people killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine
Russia chose 'Easter escalation' over ceasefire, says Zelensky

world.headline: Israel Strikes Lebanon Hospital as Ground Invasion Advances
world.body:
Israeli forces damaged a hospital in the Lebanese city of Tyre as a ground invasion pushed further into the country. The strike on a medical facility drew immediate condemnation and raised fresh questions about the rules of engagement governing Israeli operations across multiple fronts simultaneously.

Israel is now conducting active military campaigns in Gaza, Lebanon, and against Iran - a scale of simultaneous warfare with few modern precedents.

sources.title: Israeli strikes damage hospital in Lebanon’s Tyre; ground invasion advances
Israel’s unchecked war machine

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Lunar Halfway Point
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew passed the halfway mark to the Moon, releasing striking images of Earth from deep space. It is the first crewed lunar mission since Apollo 17 in 1972.

The milestone comes as the mission continues without reported issues. NASA released the photographs publicly, describing the crew's view of Earth as spectacular.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II

ai.headline: Anthropic's Busy Week: Biotech Deal, PAC, and OpenClaw Fallout
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company reportedly acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, launched a political action committee to expand its Washington presence, and is riding high in private markets - though SpaceX's anticipated IPO may compete for the same investor dollars.

Meanwhile, Anthropic cut off OpenClaw, a third-party Claude client, by requiring its users to pay extra through standard subscriptions - effectively killing the service. The move follows a serious security incident: hackers leaked Claude Code source code and bundled it with malware, and security researchers are urging OpenClaw users to assume their systems were compromised. Claude Code also made headlines for a better reason, having independently discovered a Linux vulnerability that had gone undetected for 23 years.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as AGI Chief Steps Away
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while other executives including Fidji Simo and Kate Rouch are taking on expanded responsibilities. The shuffle comes as the company pushes deeper into product and commercial territory.

Separately, OpenAI's head of AGI readiness is taking a leave of absence - a notable departure given how central that role is to the company's stated mission. No replacement has been announced. The back-to-back personnel moves suggest internal pressure as OpenAI tries to scale operations while managing its increasingly complex corporate structure.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence

ai.headline: Musk Forces Grok on Banks; Meta Hit by Data Breach
ai.body:
Elon Musk is reportedly requiring investment banks working on the SpaceX IPO to purchase Grok subscriptions - a coercive tactic that blurs the line between business leverage and product promotion. Banks eager for a role in one of the most anticipated public offerings in years appear to be complying.

At Meta, a data breach at AI hiring platform Mercor exposed sensitive industry information, prompting Meta to pause its relationship with the company. The incident adds to a difficult stretch for Meta, which is also dealing with a court-ordered gag on Sarah Wynn-Williams, author of "Careless People," barring her from making negative public statements about the company.

sources.title: Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
Meta Pauses Work With Mercor After Data Breach Puts AI Industry Secrets at Risk
Author of "Careless People" banned from saying anything negative about Meta

ai.headline: AI Is Eroding Critical Thinking, and Data Centers Are Burning Gas
ai.body:
New research finds that heavy AI users increasingly defer to model outputs without applying their own judgment - a pattern researchers are calling "cognitive surrender." The effect appears strongest among frequent users, raising questions about long-term impacts on reasoning skills.

On the infrastructure side, AI companies are racing to build dedicated natural gas plants to power data centers, bypassing the grid entirely. Critics warn of locked-in emissions for decades. Trump's parallel push for a domestic AI data center buildout is stumbling, with his own tariffs driving up hardware costs and deterring the foreign investment his plan depends on.

sources.title: "Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds
AI companies are building huge natural gas plants to power data centers. What could go wrong?
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing

btc.headline: Bitcoin Bears Face $2.5B Wipeout as Market Liquidity Thins
btc.body:
Bitcoin short sellers are sitting on $2.5 billion in positions that would be liquidated if the price climbs to $72,000, putting bears in a precarious spot. Analysts say the current directionless price action is compressing into a coil that could snap hard in either direction.

The risk is compounded by deteriorating market depth. Five separate data sources point to the same conclusion: Bitcoin's order books are thinning from the inside, meaning large moves will find less resistance and more volatility. Meanwhile, large traders had a brutal first quarter, losing an average of $337 million per day. The pain has been real, and the setup for a sharp breakout is building.

sources.title: Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside
Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst

btc.headline: Kalshi Blocked in Nevada as Prediction Markets Test Legal Limits
btc.body:
A Nevada judge extended the ban on Kalshi's sports event contracts, rejecting the platform's argument that its products are federally regulated financial instruments rather than unlicensed gambling. The ruling is a significant setback for prediction markets trying to operate across state lines under CFTC oversight.

The legal pressure is not limited to the US. Prediction markets are running into similar walls across strict Asian jurisdictions, where regulators are drawing hard lines between financial derivatives and gambling. Polymarket also took heat this week, pulling down a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash. The industry is expanding fast but regulators are catching up.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense
Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets
Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash
Judge continues Nevada ban on Kalshi sports markets

btc.headline: Bitcoin ETFs Set to Overtake Gold, Tether Eyes $500B Valuation
btc.body:
Analyst James Seyffart argues Bitcoin ETFs will eventually surpass gold ETFs in size, citing Bitcoin's expanding use cases and growing institutional appetite. Gold ETFs took decades to build their base. Bitcoin is moving faster.

Separately, Tether is weighing a fundraising round at a $500 billion valuation but may delay if investor demand falls short of expectations. The stablecoin giant's hesitation signals that even the most profitable company in crypto is not immune to market sentiment. Both stories point to the same underlying tension: institutional money is circling crypto at scale, but pricing and timing remain contested.

sources.title: Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst

btc.headline: 24/7 Stock Trading Arrives, Borrowing a Page from Crypto
btc.body:
Traditional markets are moving toward round-the-clock trading, a shift that traders say will eliminate the after-hours price gaps that have long allowed manipulation to go unchecked. The change mirrors how crypto markets have operated since inception.

For crypto, the development is both validation and competition. Bitcoin's always-on liquidity has been a core selling point over equities. As stock markets close that gap, the pressure on crypto to differentiate on other dimensions - speed, programmability, self-custody - will only grow. Solana's ongoing struggle to balance quantum-resistant security against transaction speed illustrates exactly that challenge: the next frontier of crypto's technical edge is harder to build than simply staying open on weekends.

sources.title: Traders are the big winners as 24/7 stocks will finally end the after-hours price 'manipulation'
Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory pastry with caramelized leeks and shiitake mushrooms bound by a white miso and crème fraîche filling
food.tip: Freeze your butter for 15 minutes before grating it into the flour — it creates flakier layers than cubing and cutting

exercise.name: The Saturday Deck of Cards
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards, a mat
exercise.description: Assign an exercise to each suit — push-ups, squats, hollow-body holds, and burpees — then flip cards one at a time and perform the number of reps shown, making every session unpredictably brutal and different

learn.description: Stochastic resonance is the counterintuitive phenomenon where adding a precise amount of random noise to a weak signal actually makes that signal easier to detect — used by the nervous system, crayfish to sense predators, and engineers in signal processing.
learn.why: It quietly dismantles the assumption that noise is always the enemy of clarity, and once you see it, you start noticing its fingerprints in neuroscience, climate science, and even financial markets
